title: A4 sixteen-page zine
subtitle: Folding and cutting a single A4 sheet into a sixteen-page booklet
excerpt: A single A4 sheet, folded and cut in the right sequence, produces a sixteen-page zine without any glue or staples. The only tools needed are scissors and a bone folder. This construction is the fastest entry point into zine-making and the best way to plan a zine before committing to print.
sourceNotes: Original tutorial based on the single-sheet zine construction widely taught in zine workshops and documented in community-publishing guides. The fold-and-cut construction method has been in common use since the early zine community of the 1970s.

glossaryTerms.definition: The arrangement of pages on a sheet so that they appear in the correct reading order after folding and cutting. For a sixteen-page single-sheet zine, pages 1, 16, 9, and 8 share the front face of the unfolded sheet.
A folded group of pages that forms a section of a booklet. The single-sheet zine is itself one complete signature of sixteen pages.
The single central cut made in a single-sheet zine: a cut along the fold line between pages 8 and 9 that allows the sheet to open into a cross shape and then collapse into the booklet form.

content.text: to arrange all sixteen pages on one face of an A4 sheet, plus one central
that allows the sheet to collapse into a
. Learning the page order before printing or writing is essential.

items.name: A4 paper, one sheet
items.notes: Plain white 80 gsm is the simplest to work with for a first attempt. Use a blank or printed sheet depending on whether you are planning or producing.

items.name: Pencil and ruler
items.notes: For marking the page positions before filling in content.

content.text: Fold the A4 sheet in half lengthways (landscape fold, portrait result). Sharpen the fold. Fold in half again (the top edge meets the bottom): this creates a quarter-size sheet of four layers. Sharpen. Fold in half once more (fold the right edge to meet the left): this creates an eighth-size sheet of eight layers. The result is a thick packet of eight layers. Sharpen all folds.

Unfold all the way back to the first fold (the lengthways half-fold). The sheet is now folded once, with a crease running down the centre. On the folded edge, find the midpoint (halfway along the fold) and cut from that midpoint inward along the fold for half the width of the folded sheet. This cut releases the centre section so the sheet can open into a cross.

Open the sheet completely flat. Refold lengthways. Push the two ends toward each other so the cut opens into a diamond shape. Continue pushing until the diamond closes and the pages align into a booklet. Fold all pages together and sharpen the spine fold. The booklet has sixteen pages plus a cover. Number and label each page in pencil while blank to understand the page order before producing a final version.

items.symptom: The pages are in the wrong order in the finished booklet
items.cause: The imposition was not mapped out before writing or printing.
items.fix: Always make a blank test copy first. Number the pages 1 to 16 in pencil and fold to identify the page order. Then reproduce the content in that order on the final sheet.

items.symptom: The magic cut is too long and the booklet is loose
items.cause: The cut extended beyond the centre point.
items.fix: The cut should extend exactly halfway into the folded sheet from the folded edge. If the cut is too long, reinforce the spine with a strip of washi tape on the inside.
